questions.question: Explain the concept of pipes in Unix/Linux with an example.
keyPoints: Definition of pipe as a mechanism connecting command output to input
Symbol used is vertical bar (|)
Example: ls | grep 'file' or cat file.txt | sort | uniq
Enables chaining of commands for complex operations

questions.question: List and explain three commonly used grep options with examples.
keyPoints: -i: case-insensitive search (grep -i 'hello' file)
-v: invert match (grep -v 'error' log.txt)
-n: show line numbers (grep -n 'pattern' file)
-r: recursive search
-l: list filenames only

questions.question: Explain the difference between Basic Regular Expressions (BRE) and Extended Regular Expressions (ERE).
keyPoints: BRE is the default syntax used by grep
ERE is used by egrep and supports additional metacharacters
In BRE, metacharacters like ?, +, {, }, | need escaping with \
In ERE, these metacharacters work directly without escaping
ERE provides more powerful and readable pattern matching

questions.question: What are anchors in regular expressions? Explain ^ and $ with examples.
keyPoints: Anchors match positions in text, not characters
^ (caret) matches start of line
$ matches end of line
Example: '^Hello' matches lines starting with Hello
Example: 'world$' matches lines ending with world
Example: '^$' matches empty lines

questions.question: Explain the following regex metacharacters: ., *, +, ?, []
keyPoints: . matches any single character
* matches zero or more of preceding element
+ matches one or more of preceding element (ERE)
? matches zero or one of preceding element (ERE)
[] defines character class - matches any single character in brackets
[^] negates character class

questions.question: Write a command to search for all lines containing either 'error' or 'warning' in a log file.
keyPoints: Using egrep: egrep 'error|warning' logfile.txt
Using grep -E: grep -E 'error|warning' logfile.txt
Using BRE with escaped |: grep 'error\|warning' logfile.txt
Add -i for case-insensitive: grep -Ei 'error|warning' logfile.txt

questions.question: Explain the difference between grep, egrep, and fgrep.
keyPoints: grep: uses Basic Regular Expressions
egrep: uses Extended Regular Expressions (more features)
fgrep: Fixed string search (no regex interpretation)
Modern grep supports -E (extended) and -F (fixed) options
egrep is equivalent to grep -E, fgrep is equivalent to grep -F

questions.question: Explain regular expressions and write patterns to match: a) Email addresses b) Phone numbers c) IP addresses
keyPoints: Regular expressions are patterns describing text
Explanation of each component in the pattern
May need refinement for valid ranges

questions.question: Write a shell pipeline to find the top 5 most frequently occurring words in a text file.
keyPoints: cat file.txt | tr -s ' ' '\n' | tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -5
tr -s squeezes spaces into single space
tr converts to lowercase for case-insensitive counting
sort groups identical words
uniq -c counts occurrences
sort -rn sorts numerically in reverse order
head -5 shows top 5

questions.question: Create a comprehensive regex pattern to validate Indian mobile phone numbers and explain each component.
keyPoints: Basic format: 10 digits starting with 7-9
^ anchors to start
[789] first digit must be 7, 8, or 9
[0-9]{9} remaining 9 digits
$ anchors to end
Alternative formats: +91 (country code)

questions.question: Explain the grep command family and their differences. Provide practical examples for each.
keyPoints: grep: Basic Regular Expressions, original version
egrep: Extended Regular Expressions, supports +, ?, | without escaping
fgrep: Fixed strings, fastest for literal patterns
grep -E: Modern way to use ERE (recommended over egrep)
grep -F: Modern way for fixed strings (recommended over fgrep)
Practical examples showing each use case
